Title: Learn Go with Pocket-Sized Projects
Author: Aliénor Latour
My Review:
I loved this book!

I would consider myself an intermidiate Go programmer, but I think this book would suit any Go developer, from "What is this Go everyone's talking about" to experienced Go devs.

I absolutely loved the way that each concept was introduced in a stepwise fashion, one chapter building upon the next.

Also, as a reader of technical books, I appreciate the actual writing. This writing was engaging and entertaining. It made reading a pleasure.

If there were six stars, I'd give six, but sadly I can only give five.

Title: The Lies of Locke Lamora (Gentleman Bastard, #1)
Author: Scott Lynch
My Review:
I really enjoyed this book. While I would not classify it as full on “grimdark” a la Abercrombe, it is certainly in that vein, which I love. And many times I found myself chuckling aloud at some of the characters quips or the narrator’s turns of phrase. The world-building gave me a definite sense of place and culture. We don’t see much of the rest of the world, but the port city of Camorr comes vividly to life. The setting has a distinct Renaissance Italian city-state feel, with the city nominally run by a duke, but actually run by a college of Dons.

Locke Lamora, to outward appearances, is one of thousands of Commoran street criminals. But he is so much more than that. He is secretly the the Thorn of Camorr, the most notorious con man in the city. The book starts with him and his faithful little gang planning their next con, and then shit goes sideways from there…

The format of the book runs in two threads, with the main plot supplemented by many flashbacks. I found this kept the plot moving quickly, while filling in the necessary character-forming background as necessary.

As an added bonus, for those who prefer audiobooks, the narrator Michael Page brings the characters to life in both endearing and horrifying ways.

As the blurb says, this is a murder mystery. Absolutely loved it. The characters are real. The dialog is great. The main character is sympathetic, as are many of the supporting cast.

And the ending totally caught me by surprise. Never saw it coming. I would highly recommend.

My Review:
I absolutely loved this book. Lift is one of my favorite supporting characters from the Stormlight series. She calls herself a street urchin, and acts and thinks accordingly.

She considers herself to be completely selfish but acts completly opposite, putting herself in danger to help people in distress.

I cannot recommend this book more highly.

Title: Mistborn: The Final Empire (Mistborn, #1)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review:
I loved this book the first time I read it, and loved it even more the second time around.

There's so much going on. First of all, there's the whole world Sanderson has created. As with any good fantasy/sci-fi, the key is having a consistent set of rules (however far-fetched) and then making the story work within those rules.

Then the character development is fantastic. These are people I want to know more about.

This may be the beginning of a full re-read of Mistborn. It's that good.

Title: The Book Thief
Author: Markus Zusak
My Review:
I absolutely loved this book. The language was beautiful, lyrical, and evocative. The entire premise was innovative and engaging. The story was well constructed and heart wrenching.

This is yet another recent book I have read about WWII and Nazi Germany. In our current political climate, these stories are particularly pertinent. How an otherwise civilized people can arrive at the place where they call someone giving a starving man a piece of bread a “jew lover” is just a horrific testament to the dark side of human nature and mob psychology.

I want to start this book again tonight from the beginning. I cannot recommend it highly enough.

Title: The Martian Chronicles
Author: Ray Bradbury
My Review: I had no idea what to expect from this book, and was pleasantly surprised. It's not quite a novel but more a series of loosely linked vignettes. Some had a Twilight Zone quality to them, while others had political themes. Bradbury writes in a beautiful, poetic style, and the stories are filled with evocative imagery and descriptions. Overall, a fun and thought provoking read that I would heartily recommend to any science fiction reader.

Title: All the Light We Cannot See
Author: Anthony Doerr
My Review: A horrifying glimpse into the collective madness that was Nazi Germany. Under a system of institutionalized evil, the Nazis believe they are not committing atrocities but are imposing order upon chaos. Under such a system, trying to do good can be dangerous or deadly. This is a story of those who decide to risk their lives to be human in an inhuman world.

Title: A Thousand Splendid Suns
Author: Khaled Hosseini
My Review:
I loved this book. It gives an amazing look into Afghan culture and daily life, and the position and treatment of women in that culture. It also provides an Afghan perspective on the political events of the 70s through the 2000s.

But at its core, it’s a story of love and sacrifice. It speaks to the power of love, and its ability to endure and overcome under nearly impossible circumstances. It is, in turn, infuriating, heartwarming, and heartbreaking.

I would highly recommend.

I love how one of the ancillary characters was named Suprynowicz. I watched carefully to see if the first name was Vin, but the author played that one very cagily.

Title: Starship Troopers
Author: Robert A. Heinlein
My Review:
What do you do when you have to drive up to Boston alone to pick your kid up from college? Slap a Heinlein novel into the iPod and get going.

The book is essentially a memoir, telling us the story of John Rico and his life in the mobile infantry. However, as in most Heinlein books, we get a strong dose of political theory along with the story.

I really liked this book, and I'm glad I finally got the chance to read it, as my head was stuffed with images from the horrific Starship Troopers movie. The only thing similar between the book and the movie is that they are both called Starship Trooopers and both involve John Rico. If you didn't like the movie, give the book a try. It's totally different.

Title: The Last Centurion
Author: John Ringo
My Review:
This book rocked. It was a great combination of military action and political philosophy, with a serious helping of science and technology thrown in. If you are a tofu-eater, as the main character Bandit Six calls them, you're not going to enjoy this book. It takes swipes at many "politically correct" notions, most notably global warming.

Felt like a cross between Heinlein and Clancy. It was a real page turner, but also gave me a lot to think about. I highly recommend.
